relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Dinah went out to see the daughters of the land.
Shechem saw her and harmed her, turning sight and power against her.
After the harm, his soul clung to her, and he spoke tenderly, though the wound remained.
He asked his father to secure Dinah for him as a wife.
Jacob heard and kept silent until his sons returned from the field.
Hamor came to speak with Jacob.
The brothers came in pained and furious because disgrace had been done in Israel.
Hamor tried to turn Shechem’s desire into a marriage negotiation.
He proposed intermarriage between the households.
He offered land, dwelling, trade, and holdings.
Shechem offered whatever price they named.
Bride-price and gift were placed on the table as if payment could answer harm.
Jacob’s sons answered with deceit because Dinah had been dishonored.
They made circumcision the condition for any union.
They demanded that every male become like them.
Then, they said, the peoples could become one.
If not, they would take Dinah and go.
The words pleased Hamor and Shechem.
Shechem moved quickly because he desired Jacob’s daughter and held honor in his house.
Hamor and Shechem brought the proposal to the city gate.
They presented Jacob’s family as peaceful and useful for land and trade.
Circumcision became the condition for becoming one people.
They persuaded the city by pointing to wealth that could become theirs.
The men of the city listened and accepted the condition.
On the third day, Simeon and Levi moved in retaliation.
They struck Hamor and Shechem and took Dinah out of Shechem’s house.
Jacob’s sons plundered the city because of what had been done to their sister.
Flocks, herds, donkeys, city, and field were taken.
The retaliation spread over households and wealth, becoming collective devastation.
Jacob feared they had made him hated among the peoples of the land.
The brothers answered with the question of Dinah’s dignity.
